summary: Nadia Asparouhova announces her new book 'Antimemetics: Why Some Ideas Resist Spreading,' inspired by qntm's science fiction novel 'There Is No Antimemetics Division.' She defines antimemes as self-censoring ideas including taboos, cognitive biases, and memory lapses that resist being shared despite their importance. The book examines how the social web is shifting from public virality toward private and semi-private spaces like group chats and DMs, where the most interesting ideas now circulate. She frames this as a nonfiction treatment of antimemetics grounded in real-world phenomena, connecting it to her previous work on open source sustainability. The book is published through the Dark Forest Collective, which she sees as fitting given that antimemes would be crushed by conventional publishing's virality-driven approach. She positions the work as a sequel of sorts to 'Working in Public,' arguing that the withdrawal from public spaces she observed among open source developers has now become universal.
key_points: Antimemes are a broad category of self-censoring ideas—taboos, cognitive biases, memory lapses—that resist being remembered or shared despite their importance
The social web is shifting from public virality toward private spaces: group chats, invite-only newsletters, and private apps now carry the most interesting ideas
Group chats are inherently antimemetic—we can only know what we've experienced ourselves, making them nearly impossible to write about publicly
Many people feel conflicted between wanting to withdraw from the chaos of public social media and feeling guilty about disengaging from public discourse
The pattern Asparouhova observed among open source developers retreating from public attention in 'Working in Public' has now become a broader societal phenomenon
money_quotes: It's easier than ever to share ideas. But in recent years, some of the most interesting ideas are burrowing deeper underground, circulating quietly among group chats and texts instead of public social channels.

Memes may dominate how we communicate online, but in the last few years, their rapid spread has also necessitated the birth of a parallel, shadow ecosystem, where the best ideas are paradoxically harder to find.
Chasing virality is no longer a sufficient motive to explain why we do (or don't) share what we do online. But we still don't have the vocabulary to describe what we're doing instead.
It's much more comfortable to live in our group chats and DMs, but doesn't it feel a little strange to watch the world burn from the safety of our miniaturized abodes?

The past is the past: we need to let it go. Instead, I wanted to take a hard, unflinching look at where we are today, and try to figure out how to make meaning from our lives with the constraints we currently have.

Antimemes are delicate and messy and hard to look at; they would be swiftly crushed in the hands of a publisher trying to juice it up and make it 'go viral,' like a flower ground under the heel of a shoe.

I wrote that book partly because I felt that what was happening to open source developers – who, in the face of overwhelming demand for their attention, were withdrawing from public online spaces – was going to happen to all of us.
key_insight: The rapid spread of memes has paradoxically created a shadow ecosystem of antimemes—ideas that resist sharing—driving the most interesting discourse underground into private spaces, and we lack the vocabulary to understand this shift.
